item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from Getty Images and requires purchase for official use. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love gimmicks, and I wasn't expecting that. You surprised me and that, while not particularly hard to do, isn't easy either. So I'll send an upvote your way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one who checked the page source for the full sonnet?

Also, I'm not as jaded about the username trick as some people, but I don't think it's particularly well applied here.

I do like the basic idea, but I'm not sold on the execution. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would love this IRL If it didn't make me not want to talk IRL, if it didn't make me talk in iambic pentameter, and if it didn't put me in a coma (basically, if I could have a gland in brain that let me communicate with the internet :D, although I bet most people would like that as well).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The username isn't actually counted as part of the verse; I suppose I could put it on the next line to make that more clear. Also, yeah, "within" doesn't really work that well. I'm not sure what to use instead, maybe "inside."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is already five feet before the username, so it doesn't ever scan. Am I missing something?

So close/ your eyes/, and op/en those/ within/, [username]

Also that fifth foot "within" seems trochaic to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is justified in-universe, yes. But so are a vast majority of the instances where it's used. And this kind of thing is plausible, yes. But the leadup to it seems really brief and quick so there isn't much time to really buy into it.

I do love the rest of this article, which is why I didn't downvote it. And obviously there are enough people upvoting this who like or love the module gimmick that it's probably not worth removing in the long-run just for a few stubborn upvoters. But it doesn't work here for me, and I've been very picky about when I can buy into the disbelief enough to go with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The preposition thing is an attempt to apply Latin grammar to English. Or as Churchill said, “This is the sort of bloody nonsense up with which I will not put.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have endlessly debated whether to keep that there or not. I've always felt that it was in line with the nature of the entity, since (in-universe) it only affects people with accounts who are logged in (yuck, preposition). It just makes sense to me that it would pull your account name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am so not a fan of the username module thing, particularly here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So close your eyes, and open those within, William See.

And that got an up vote.

item.title: My First Comment
item.content:encoded: Have my first comment for all the hard work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having reached this from another post that talked about examples using the username thingy, I could see the end coming from like the beginning of the description. So, huh, the impact was low. Guess that's the danger from spoilers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting idea; memetic poems.
Yet an ending that feels a bit too gimmicky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was one of those "this name just shouts keter" and it was kind of things. sadly the hook wasnt enough for an upvote. it's interesting but not enough to get either vote direction

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That "insert-username" schtick can really only be done once, and I've already seen it done much better. On the original, it scared the piss out of me, but when I read this, I already knew it was a thing, so my only reaction was "Really? You had to copy the one thing than can't properly be duplicated?" I imagine if I had read this first, I might have had the same reaction I had when I first saw the username-trick, but since I saw it before and saw it better, this one really doesn't do it for me. I'd suggest finding your own spine-tingler twist instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering my name is a reference to Shakespearian text, the name drop worked really well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. The ending is gimmicky. Upvoted anyway but still, it didn't even capitalize my name at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice script, but it kind of killed the atmosphere when it called my schifter. Still, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

First time reading the article. Not quite where I thought it was heading, and definitely a strong, unique, interesting anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has been updated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's more fun that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remove the censoring of the percentage, I particularly find no purpose for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I don't think that worked. I've reverted the article back to it's original form, but removed all "memetic" references.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The iambic feet don't scan particularly well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: edit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure yet if the new Interview Log is a detraction or bonus to this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm, I'd think that the Foundation would try to communicate with stage II infectees over the Internet in order to ask them about "the Beloved".

SCP-1769 is a 14-line section of non-functional script capable of infecting networking-based websites;

Wait, what would a non-networking-based website be?

These vocalizations tend to occur in iambic pentameter and describe an entity called "The Beloved" that the subject interacts with online.

Isn't iambic pentameter an English language thing? What happens if the infected isn't fluent in English?

Specific nature of vocalizations at this stage are unknown due to their memetic properties.

Couldn't they try using voice recognition software on the vocalizations?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Human computer virus is rather well-trodden territory, but this is an adequate treatment of the trope. I'm edged into an upvote by the router-tumors. I don't think I've seen anything quite like that before, and I find the idea intriguing and a bit thought-provoking. It was exactly the dash of originality needed to save this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the new image, good job on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I'm the kind of guy who decides how good it's an article depending of the hook…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty solid rewrite, have an upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I'm actually kind of flattered that people are arguing over my article. I get your point, Dr. Guin, a memetic computer virus is not exactly original, but an old idea can be rewritten into a clever work if something interesting enough is intertwined. It's not the virus that's supposed to be interesting, it's what the virus implies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'd rather have a picture of the containment sector but I haven't found a suitable image yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I don't want to give away too much of the mystery about "The Beloved", but it's rather important to the main idea of the article that the infection targets humans, because it's supposed to literally draw them into the internet from the physical world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized I want this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-623343

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, and the containment procedure is well written. However, I feel that it's lacking a specific grab. The symptoms are severely lacking in that department. The idea of something as archaic as a sonnet that spreads through something as modern as the internet tickles my fancy. The human infection, however, disappointed me. I was hoping for it to do something crazy to the computer, or maybe the environment around it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just find this one extremely cumbersome both in its effects and how they're delivered. Not bad, but very slow building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coming up next: An unkillable lizard, a statue that moves when you don't look at it, and my proving that you can make any SCP sound bad by phrasing it right and therefore your argument is invalid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, in a bout of dumb, I tried a computer virus that spreads through lolcats the day before yesterday. x3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a computer virus that infects people, except it's spread through a sonnet.

Coming up next week: a computer virus that infects people except it's spread through a picture of a tiger, then a computer virus that infects people except it's spread through jaunty limericks, then a computer virus that infects people except it's spread through a David Bowie music video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you look at an otherwise okay article, and read a line that makes you go "holy shit, that's [Adjective here]," and it makes you like the article or want to come back and read it again, or (my personal favorite) reread the entire thing with a new sense of "oh, god"… That's a hook.

In other words, give us a reason to feel some sort of emotional impact about the article. Make us laugh. Make us cry. Make us piss our pants. Surprise us with some unseen (yet logical) consequence. Make us think about your article well after we finish reading it.

Failing that, an interesting image is always fun to work with. XD

Hope this helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hook is something that draws the reader in and makes it memorable, like how 682 is unkillable (kinda bad example, but eh) and so doing an article without a good hook can be tough because some people decide their votes based on whether or not they like the hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this rewrite. It sounds plausible and is written well. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, then it's simply not my cup of tea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, perhaps an interview with an infected individual might make it more effective. One of the angles I was trying to work from is the implication that just one instance of SCP-1769-A could infect millions of people if it reached Stage V.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When inquired about this, they will respond as though this is normal, and describe other forms of communication as feeling "strange" or "disconnected".

I dislike the use of quotes here, because you can just state that they have a dissociative response to other forms of communication, or something.

Other than that I… really don't care about it. It's not bad it just does nothing for me. Possibly because I'm sitting here going "so its a virus that makes people crazy big woop, make me feel the love for the enigmatic beloved".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there anything in particular you'd like to note?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would you define "hook"? I know it's supposed to draw the reader in, but the definition is always so vague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what I like about it, there's no big hook. That may hurt it in the long run, but it feels kind of like a refreshing change to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, this is better. It actually does something interesting and while it has no proper hook, I think the improvement is considerable. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was what caught my eye. It's almost a whole new article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite the turnaround on this. The poem aspect caught me immediately, and I think that alone makes this stand above other "computer virus what infects meatpeople" skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pulling off "an 835" is a bit tricky :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving the change to radio waves. Works much better now. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sonnet portion was expunged from the original article; I had originally intended to do the same thing 835 did with releasing expunged materials, but this works much better. Thanks for your support, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now it works, kinda. I'd actually be happier if you made it removable, just with the mechanical difficulty of removing something that's growing from the fucking hypothalamus.Kinda like a brain tumour

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, because I truly love the idea of an internet-based sonnet messing with your mind and brain. I also think that this gives just the right amount of information about the nature of the anomaly (that is, very little, but enough to intrigue).
